---
title: 'The Dreaded Register | Preventing overdrafts, by actually knowing your balance... the real balance.'
date: '2016-05-08T06:46:00.0010000+00:00'
draft: true
blogger_blog: 'Darrell Wolfe, Storyteller'
blogger_status: 'LIVE'
blogger_post_id: '2238438747870706818'
blogger_filename: '/2016/05/the-dreaded-register-preventing_7.html'
blogger_source: 'takeout-20260223T235336Z-3-001.zip:Takeout/Blogger/Blogs/Darrell Wolfe, Storyteller/feed.atom'
categories:
  - 'Money'
---

<h4><br /></h4><table align="center" cellpadding="0" cellspacing="0" class="tr-caption-container" style="margin-left: auto; margin-right: auto; text-align: center;"><tbody><tr><td style="text-align: center;"><a href="/images/imported/blogger-googleusercontent-com/heartlover1717-check-register-3341c8f92110.jpg" imageanchor="1" style="margin-left: auto; margin-right: auto;"><img border="0" height="300" src="/images/imported/blogger-googleusercontent-com/heartlover1717-check-register-92daad00008a.jpg" width="400" /></a></td></tr><tr><td class="tr-caption" style="text-align: center;"><a href="https://www.flickr.com/photos/heartlover1717/8065584934/in/photolist-dhJepd-duRJzW-8VtvCA-8b6Wq7-a9HEM6-fdi6Bs-9nntzf-7HZ4zt-64fGf6-g9dQdg-9gAKEo-eDLYLa-8xxtH3-9iHHQM-nqAcdP-nGMycP-a9HEsk-9iHHG6-9iLRMs-e5bLEZ-8B5H37-ag3gnE-4TJiPn-31ZPEy-dHsAD9-iat43T-ioHzg5-fdiTtG-fd4mtB-cwFfB3-fdi6rL-62Yv2h-5Es3vn-83U7ML-iRpH6N-pmPz1-nC4WJY-7Gx3uV-7sKMuG-nH5rVv-a9LrWf-cwFbSU-gCE23L-9gzMeZ-rNw91C-bF4wo8-4tZxHR-iasBMK-bdXUP-7pTjg6" target="_blank">Heartlover1717 Check Register...</a></td></tr></tbody></table><h4><br /></h4><h4>You CAN avoid ever paying an overdraft fee, for the rest of your life... with this easy step:</h4><br /><div class="MsoNormal">If you are not one to understand detailed facts let me just head off all Iâ€™m about to say with one simple rule to avoid overdrafts for the rest of your life.&nbsp;</div><div class="MsoNormal"><br /></div><blockquote class="tr_bq"><i>The bottom line is this: keep a separate written record and donâ€™t spend more than you have.</i>&nbsp;</blockquote><br />Now for those who need more letâ€™s take a deeper look at that statement.&nbsp; <br /><div class="MsoNormal"><br /></div><h4>~Record~</h4><div class="MsoNormal"><o:p></o:p></div><div class="MsoNormal"><br /></div><div class="MsoNormal">A separate written record is one in which you write everything down from the largest payment to the smallest cup of soda. Using your online banking, as great a tool as it is does not excuse you from keeping a record separate from the bank. You can use paper and pen, or a software program like Quicken, or even build your own excel sheet. If you do use Quicken I donâ€™t advise downloading your history, input all the data manually.&nbsp;</div><div class="MsoNormal"><br /></div><div class="MsoNormal">Downloading data can make it harder to catch fraudulent transactions. More than one person has called their bank about a small transaction only to find out itâ€™s been coming out for two years or more without their knowledge. <o:p></o:p></div><div class="MsoNormal"><br /></div><div class="MsoNormal">However you do it, you must track the transactions going in and out of your account separate from the bank. This is the only way to know for sure that no fraud is going on and find out what your balance is.&nbsp;</div><div class="MsoNormal"><br /></div><blockquote class="tr_bq"><b><i>The bank doesn't know your balance.&nbsp;</i></b></blockquote><div class="MsoNormal"><br /></div><div class="MsoNormal">There are many reasons why your real balance may not be the same as the balance showing online.&nbsp;</div><div class="MsoNormal"><br /></div><div class="MsoNormal">Online (or at the ATM, 800 Number, or any other bank record) only reflects that which the bank has received not everything you have done. Even card transactions do not always come through right away.&nbsp;</div><div class="MsoNormal"><br /></div><div class="MsoNormal">I actually heard someone once say totally serious:&nbsp;</div><div class="MsoNormal"><br /></div><blockquote class="tr_bq"><i>â€œHow could I be overdrawn? I still have checks.â€&nbsp;</i></blockquote><div class="MsoNormal"><br /></div><div class="MsoNormal">This is a humorous but true example of a total lack of understanding how money and math works. This person should not have a bank account, or even handle money. They should have someone take care of things for them. <o:p></o:p></div><div class="MsoNormal"><br /></div><div class="MsoNormal"><br /></div><h4>~How?~</h4><div class="MsoNormal"><o:p></o:p></div><div class="MsoNormal"><br /></div><div class="MsoNormal">Keeping a record does take work. One of the first things you must understand; just because you deposit a check into the bank doesnâ€™t mean itâ€™s available to use.&nbsp;</div><div class="MsoNormal"><br /></div><div class="MsoNormal">Checks usually have to post to the account before you can use them. Also, checks may have holds placed on them, or even be returned as no good. If you have any doubt that the check you put in is good, wait a full three weeks before using the funds.&nbsp;</div><div class="MsoNormal"><br /></div><div class="MsoNormal">That is how long it can take for a bank to find out that the check was no good (Yes even in 2011). That being said the first step, assuming that you start with a zero balance, is to input your deposits. You should not spend what you do not have. <o:p></o:p></div><div class="MsoNormal"><br /></div><h4>~Available Balance~</h4><div class="MsoNormal"><o:p></o:p></div><div class="MsoNormal">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;</div><div class="MsoNormal">What is my ledger balance VS my available balance?</div><div class="MsoNormal"><br /></div><div class="MsoNormal"><o:p></o:p></div><div class="MsoNormal">When items post the result is a balance of some kind. This is called your â€œLedger Balanceâ€ also known as your balance as of the last business day. It is the ending balance including everything that has officially posted but not the balance including any pending transactions (those that have not yet posted).<o:p></o:p></div><div class="MsoNormal">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;</div><div class="MsoNormal">Your available balance is the Ledger Balance adding/subtracting anything showing as pending. As we said with checks there are instances where a deposit that is pending is not part of the available balance either. Unless the deposit was electronic it wonâ€™t usually be available until it has posted.&nbsp;</div><div class="MsoNormal"><br /></div><div class="MsoNormal">There are also lots of ways for your card transactions to not&nbsp;always&nbsp;show as pending all the time. Sometimes transactions especially card transactions may double or triple pend, pend for wrong amounts (more or less), pend for several days, then disappear and post at a later date. <o:p></o:p></div><div class="MsoNormal"><br /></div><div class="MsoNormal">Therefore always check your balance on a&nbsp;separate&nbsp;written record where you write everything down and add and subtract as you go. Not only is this a good idea, you are required to according to the bank in your disclosures. Calling for your balance, going online,&nbsp;ATM, or in any other way contacting your bank for your balance is not a replacement for a written record. If you contact the bank for your balance assume itâ€™s wrong!</div><div class="MsoNormal"><o:p></o:p></div><div class="MsoNormal">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;</div><div class="MsoNormal">Always look to your own records for up to date information. If you donâ€™t know your balance without contacting the bank, than no-one does especially the bank. <o:p></o:p></div><div class="MsoNormal"><br /></div><h4>~Fees~</h4><div class="MsoNormal"><o:p></o:p></div><div class="MsoNormal">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;</div><div class="MsoNormal">Overdraft fees are awful, high, outrageous, expensive, andâ€¦ well you get the point. You know they are&nbsp;awful. I once had one item go through my account and cause several items to post into overdraft causing, more items to post into a negative balance and so forth. I paid over $450 just in fees that one month. I donâ€™t know about you, but I didnâ€™t have $450 to spend on fees, so it put me behind months. <o:p></o:p></div><div class="MsoNormal">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;</div><div class="MsoNormal">Do you know who was to blame for that overdraft causing $450 in fees though? The Bank? The President? The Congress? No. It was me. I set up the payment and agreed to have it auto deducted. I failed to account for it in my balancing. I did it, knowing full well the fees that could come if the balance was not there for it when it came. Therefore I am the only person to blame. The sooner you get a hold of that reality the sooner you can take action to prevent another fee on your account.<o:p></o:p></div><div class="MsoNormal"><br /></div><h4>~Winds of Change~</h4><div class="MsoNormal"><o:p></o:p></div><div class="MsoNormal"><br /></div><div class="MsoNormal">On July 21st of 2010 a banking regulation was signed into law by president Obama. It is called the Dodd-Frank Wall Street Reform and Consumer Protection Act<a href="https://d.docs.live.net/6a09c1440da84011/Wolfe%20Family%20Conceptions%20Projects/!!Writing/Banking%20Basics/Website/Banking%20Basics%20for%20Believers%20stripped.doc#_ftn1" name="_ftnref1" title=""><!--[if !supportFootnotes]--><span style="color: black; font-family: &quot;times new roman&quot; , serif; font-size: 12.0pt;">[1]</span><!--[endif]--></a>. Immediately following this actsâ€™ passing banks were limited to how many overdraft fees could be charged per day and how they could approve and post items that could cause fees. Each bank drafted new policies around this regulation. Those two changes were only the beginning. As of the beginning of 2011 there are still 900 laws that still havenâ€™t been written from the stipulations of that act. The financial picture is changing. Banks are being restricted and so are the free services. What does this mean to you and your overdrafts? It may be a small bit harder to get a fee, but a great deal harder to ask the bank to reverse it. <o:p></o:p></div><div class="MsoNormal"><br /></div><h4>~Accounting 101~</h4><div class="MsoNormal"><o:p></o:p></div><div class="MsoNormal">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;</div><div class="MsoNormal">The only sure fire way to avoid an overdraft fee isâ€¦ drum roll pleaseâ€¦ to not overdraw. I know, magic right? Allow me to review accounting 101. Anyone can do this.<o:p></o:p></div><div class="MsoNormal">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;</div><div class="MsoNormal">The correct way to use your account is to make a deposit (or add money) to your account. Then make withdrawals less than or equal to the amount deposited including any fees or charges.<o:p></o:p><br /><br /></div><div class="MsoNormal"><br /></div><div class="separator" style="clear: both; text-align: center;"><a href="/images/imported/blogger-googleusercontent-com/image010-f1695fb77834.jpg" imageanchor="1" style="margin-left: 1em; margin-right: 1em;"><img border="0" height="100" src="/images/imported/blogger-googleusercontent-com/image010-b02d6e87ed89.jpg" width="320" /></a></div><div class="separator" style="clear: both; text-align: center;"><br /></div><div class="MsoNormal"><br /></div><div class="MsoNormal"><!--[if gte vml 1]><v:shapetype id="_x0000_t75" coordsize="21600,21600"  o:spt="75" o:preferrelative="t" path="m@4@5l@4@11@9@11@9@5xe" filled="f"  stroked="f"> <v:stroke joinstyle="miter"/> <v:formulas>  <v:f eqn="if lineDrawn pixelLineWidth 0"/>  <v:f eqn="sum @0 1 0"/>  <v:f eqn="sum 0 0 @1"/>  <v:f eqn="prod @2 1 2"/>  <v:f eqn="prod @3 21600 pixelWidth"/>  <v:f eqn="prod @3 21600 pixelHeight"/>  <v:f eqn="sum @0 0 1"/>  <v:f eqn="prod @6 1 2"/>  <v:f eqn="prod @7 21600 pixelWidth"/>  <v:f eqn="sum @8 21600 0"/>  <v:f eqn="prod @7 21600 pixelHeight"/>  <v:f eqn="sum @10 21600 0"/> </v:formulas> <v:path o:extrusionok="f" gradientshapeok="t" o:connecttype="rect"/> <o:lock v:ext="edit" aspectratio="t"/></v:shapetype><v:shape id="_x0000_i1025" type="#_x0000_t75" style='width:4in;  height:84pt' o:bordertopcolor="this" o:borderleftcolor="this"  o:borderbottomcolor="this" o:borderrightcolor="this"> <v:imagedata src="file:///C:\Users\store\AppData\Local\Temp\msohtmlclip1\01\clip_image001.jpg"   o:title="Accounting 101"/> <w:bordertop type="thinThickSmall" width="24"/> <w:borderleft type="thinThickSmall" width="24"/> <w:borderbottom type="thickThinSmall" width="24"/> <w:borderright type="thickThinSmall" width="24"/></v:shape><![endif]--><!--[if !vml]--><!--[endif]--><o:p></o:p></div><div class="MsoNormal">So now the balance is $36.50. However in the real world most people donâ€™t do things the way they are supposed to. The banks post items according to regulations. Your bank isn't making these rules, people in the Federal and State Governments are.<br /><br />Even different states may do things differently. Generally what happens is someone gets to the point above and then writes a check on Thursday expecting that the check wonâ€™t come through until the direct deposit comes in Friday.&nbsp; <o:p></o:p><br /><br /></div><div class="MsoNormal">Usually this works but since companies have so many convenient electronic ways of clearing checks it could come in on Thursday.<o:p></o:p><br /><br /></div><div class="MsoNormal"><br /></div><div class="separator" style="clear: both; text-align: center;"><a href="/images/imported/blogger-googleusercontent-com/image011-d0b38bafc1aa.jpg" imageanchor="1" style="margin-left: 1em; margin-right: 1em;"><img border="0" height="143" src="/images/imported/blogger-googleusercontent-com/image011-91e4532c58d8.jpg" width="320" /></a></div><div class="MsoNormal"><br /></div><div class="MsoNormal"><!--[if gte vml 1]><v:shape id="_x0000_i1026" type="#_x0000_t75"  style='width:4in;height:124.5pt' o:bordertopcolor="this" o:borderleftcolor="this"  o:borderbottomcolor="this" o:borderrightcolor="this"> <v:imagedata src="file:///C:\Users\store\AppData\Local\Temp\msohtmlclip1\01\clip_image003.jpg"   o:title="Accounting 101"/> <w:bordertop type="thinThickSmall" width="24"/> <w:borderleft type="thinThickSmall" width="24"/> <w:borderbottom type="thickThinSmall" width="24"/> <w:borderright type="thickThinSmall" width="24"/></v:shape><![endif]--><!--[if !vml]--><!--[endif]--><o:p></o:p></div><div class="MsoNormal"><br />This causes an overdraft.&nbsp;</div><div class="MsoNormal"><br /></div><div class="MsoNormal">Some banks can take your check, and clear your other bank on the same day. So you now understand how this happens.&nbsp;</div><div class="MsoNormal"><br /></div><div class="MsoNormal"></div><ul><li>What if you are already in the middle of this?&nbsp;</li></ul><ul><li>What if you are overdrawn so often you canâ€™t see how to get out of the cycle?&nbsp;</li></ul><ul><li>What do you do now if you are overdrawn right now?&nbsp;</li></ul><br /><div class="MsoNormal"><br /></div><div class="MsoNormal">Turn to the next chapter for <a href="http://bankingbasicsforbelievers.blogspot.com/2016/05/help-ive-fallen-and-i-cant-get-out-of.html" target="_blank">help</a>.&nbsp; <o:p></o:p></div><div class="MsoNormal"><br /></div><br /><div><!--[if !supportFootnotes]--><br clear="all" /><hr align="left" size="1" width="33%" /><!--[endif]--> <br /><div id="ftn1"><div class="MsoNormal" style="margin-left: 14.15pt; text-indent: -14.15pt;"><a href="https://d.docs.live.net/6a09c1440da84011/Wolfe%20Family%20Conceptions%20Projects/!!Writing/Banking%20Basics/Website/Banking%20Basics%20for%20Believers%20stripped.doc#_ftnref1" name="_ftn1" title=""><span class="MsoFootnoteReference"><!--[if !supportFootnotes]--><span class="MsoFootnoteReference"><span style="color: black; font-family: &quot;times new roman&quot; , serif; font-size: 12.0pt;">[1]</span></span><!--[endif]--></span></a><span style="font-size: 10.0pt;"> </span><span style="font-size: 8.0pt;">http://en.wikipedia.org</span><o:p></o:p></div><div class="MsoNormal" style="margin-left: 14.15pt; text-indent: -14.15pt;"><span style="font-size: 8.0pt;"><br /></span></div><div class="MsoNormal" style="margin-left: 14.15pt; text-indent: -14.15pt;"><span style="font-size: 8.0pt;"><br /></span><span style="font-size: 8.0pt;"><br /></span><span style="font-size: 8.0pt;"></span><br /><div class="MsoNormal" style="-webkit-text-stroke-width: 0px; color: black; font-family: 'Times New Roman'; font-size: medium; font-style: normal; font-variant: normal; font-weight: normal; letter-spacing: normal; line-height: normal; orphans: auto; text-align: start; text-indent: 0px; text-transform: none; white-space: normal; widows: 1; word-spacing: 0px;"></div><br /><div class="MsoNormal" style="-webkit-text-stroke-width: 0px; color: black; font-family: 'Times New Roman'; font-size: medium; font-style: normal; font-variant: normal; font-weight: normal; letter-spacing: normal; line-height: normal; orphans: auto; text-align: start; text-indent: 0px; text-transform: none; white-space: normal; widows: 1; word-spacing: 0px;"><div style="margin: 0px;"><b>The Insider</b></div><div style="margin: 0px;"><br /></div><h4>The Insider is a BIG fan of Dave Ramsey:</h4><ul><li>Go read/listen/watch Dave Ramsey and he'll teach you how to do it right!</li></ul><ul><li>Listen to The Dave Ramsey Show (<a href="http://www.daveramsey.com/show/radio/" target="_blank">HERE</a>)</li></ul><ul><li>Or by his most popular book:&nbsp;<a href="http://www.amazon.com/gp/product/1595555277/ref=as_li_tl?ie=UTF8&amp;camp=1789&amp;creative=390957&amp;creativeASIN=1595555277&amp;linkCode=as2&amp;tag=your0f4d-20&amp;linkId=EH57Y7XH3LRAVQMF" rel="" target="_blank">(HERE) The Total Money Makeover: Classic Edition: A Proven Plan for Financial Fitness</a><img alt="" border="0" src="http://ir-na.amazon-adsystem.com/e/ir?t=your0f4d-20&amp;l=as2&amp;o=1&amp;a=1595555277" height="1" style="border: none; cursor: move; margin: 0px;" width="1" /></li></ul></div></div></div></div>
